items.q: Which macronutrient provides the most energy per gram?
items.why: Students assume carbs (the 'energy food') provide most energy per gram. Carbs and protein = 4 kcal/g; FAT = 9 kcal/g (most energy-dense). Vitamins provide no energy at all.

items.correct: A protein containing all nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts.
items.misconception: A protein with the most calories.
items.random_wrong: Any protein from animal sources only.
items.why: Students think 'complete' means 'high quantity' or 'animal'. It means containing all ESSENTIAL amino acids the body can't make. Most animal proteins are complete; many plants need combining (e.g., rice + beans).

items.q: Why are dried foods less prone to spoilage?
items.correct: Low water activity prevents microorganisms from growing.
items.misconception: Bacteria do not like dry textures.
items.why: Students think it's about texture preference. Microbes need available water. Drying reduces WATER ACTIVITY below the threshold microbes can use - no water = no metabolism = no spoilage.

items.q: Which is an HBV protein source?
items.why: It's Egg. “Rice” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Slowly and steadily. “Instantly” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: UK daily fibre recommendation for adults in grams?
items.why: The real figure is 30. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
